skill.name: when-developing-ml-models-use-ml-expert
skill.description: Guides machine learning model development from data preparation through validation and deployment.
skill.summary: A structured workflow for training, evaluating, and packaging ML models.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown backticks as Ruby shell execution and several ordinary words as weak cryptography; those are false positives after context review. The skill is not malicious, but it does recommend an unpinned alpha CLI command and includes ML examples that write files and load serialized model artifacts, so publication should include a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Unpinned Alpha CLI Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: README.md instructs users to run npx claude-flow@alpha. This is a user-initiated command, not hidden execution, but alpha package resolution can change over time and may execute newly published package code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly present in a bash quick-start block. The risk is moderate because execution is explicit and user-controlled, but the alpha package reference is not pinned to an immutable version.

medium_findings.title: Serialized ML Artifact Loading in Generated Inference Code
medium_findings.description: The generated inference class loads a model path and a scaler path, including joblib.load on scaler_path. Loading serialized ML artifacts from untrusted sources can execute unsafe code or load tampered models.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly loads serialized artifacts from constructor-supplied paths. The surrounding workflow creates those artifacts locally, so the issue is a realistic caution rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Fixed Relative File Writes in Example Workflow
low_findings.description: The workflow writes training history, reports, model files, and deployment files to fixed relative paths. This is normal for ML examples, but it can overwrite local project artifacts if copied without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and limited to ML output artifacts. They are not evidence of data theft or persistence, but they are a practical local safety concern.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick and Weak-Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Most static external-command alerts came from Markdown code fences or inline documentation. The weak-cryptography alerts at the cited lines do not show MD5, SHA1, or other cryptographic API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations contain documentation, model architecture text, or distributed-training settings rather than cryptographic functions or Ruby shell execution. This strongly supports dismissal of those static findings.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx Execution of Alpha Dependency
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick-start command executes claude-flow through npx using the alpha tag. Users should pin and verify the package before running it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly visible and can execute package code. It remains user-initiated, which keeps the severity below high.

dangerous_patterns.title: Deserialization-Like Loading of ML Artifacts
dangerous_patterns.description: The inference example loads model and preprocessing artifacts from paths. Users should only load artifacts they created or verified.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The use of joblib.load is clear. The risk depends on whether the artifact path can point to untrusted files.

content.user_title: Build Production ML Models with Expert Guidance
content.value_statement: ML projects can stall when data preparation, training, validation, and deployment are handled separately. This skill provides a clear workflow for building and packaging models.
actual_capabilities: Outlines a five-phase ML workflow from data preparation to deployment.
Provides Python examples for splitting, normalizing, and encoding datasets.
Shows TensorFlow model definitions for DNN and CNN architectures.
Defines training callbacks, checkpointing, and training-history capture.
Includes evaluation reporting with classification metrics and confusion matrices.
Shows model export to Keras, SavedModel, TFLite, and a deployment folder.
limitations: Examples require adaptation for each dataset, target column, and task type.
Several code snippets are illustrative and reference variables defined elsewhere.
The skill does not provide production monitoring, drift detection, or governance controls.
Distributed training depends on optional Flow-Nexus tooling and local infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Train a First Production Candidate
use_cases.description: Create a structured path from a raw dataset to a validated model package.

use_cases.title: Standardize Model Delivery
use_cases.description: Use the same preparation, training, evaluation, and export checklist across projects.

use_cases.title: Review Deployment Readiness
use_cases.description: Check that metrics, model files, inference scripts, and documentation are produced before release.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an ML Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new dataset.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan an ML workflow for my dataset. Identify the task type, data preparation steps, model options, validation metrics, and deployment outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Training Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design preprocessing for my dataset. Include missing-value handling, categorical encoding, train-validation-test splitting, normalization, and checks for leakage.

prompt_templates.title: Design and Train a Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to recommend an architecture and training configuration. Include layers, optimizer, loss, callbacks, checkpointing, and evaluation metrics for my task.

prompt_templates.title: Package a Model for Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a deployment plan for my trained model. Include export formats, preprocessing artifacts, inference interface, validation report, and release checklist.

output_examples.input: I have a labeled tabular dataset and need a deployable classifier.
output_examples.output: A phased plan covering data cleaning, stratified splitting, DNN training, classification metrics, saved artifacts, and deployment documentation.

output_examples.input: My model is overfitting after a few epochs.
output_examples.output: A review of validation curves, dropout and regularization options, early stopping settings, and data-split checks.

output_examples.input: I need to hand off a trained model to an API team.
output_examples.output: A deployment package checklist with model files, preprocessing artifacts, inference behavior, requirements, metrics, and known limitations.

best_practices: Pin external package versions before running CLI commands from the skill examples.
Keep raw data, derived datasets, model artifacts, and deployment packages in separate directories.
Validate model artifacts and preprocessing files before loading them in inference code.
anti_patterns: Do not copy the example snippets without adapting paths, variables, and task assumptions.
Do not load model or scaler files from untrusted sources.
Do not rely only on accuracy when class imbalance or business costs matter.

faq.question: What type of ML work is this skill for?
faq.answer: It supports supervised model development workflows, especially training, validation, and packaging for deployment.

faq.question: Does the skill train models automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides structured guidance and example code that a user or agent must adapt and run.

faq.question: Which frameworks are shown?
faq.answer: The examples mainly use TensorFlow, Keras, scikit-learn, pandas, NumPy, matplotlib, and joblib.

faq.question: Can it support distributed training?
faq.answer: Yes, it includes an optional distributed-training section that depends on Flow-Nexus tooling.

faq.question: What security precautions are recommended?
faq.answer: Pin external package versions, avoid untrusted serialized artifacts, and review file paths before running examples.

faq.question: Is it ready for regulated production use?
faq.answer: Not by itself. Add governance, monitoring, model-risk review, privacy checks, and compliance controls.
